title: Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism
subtitle: Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a [terrorist attack](#dod:terrorism).
caption:
Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism

Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a terrorist attack.

This scatter plot compares the share of people worried about terrorism against the share of deaths from terrorism for various countries, with data points color-coded by continent. The vertical axis measures the share of people worried, ranging from 30% to over 90%, while the horizontal axis plots the share of deaths from terrorism on a logarithmic scale from 0.0001% to 1%. Circle sizes correspond to the number of attacks, with reference markers for 200 and 500 attacks. Iraq and Nigeria appear in the upper right quadrant, indicating both high shares of deaths (approaching or exceeding 1%) and high levels of worry (around 90%). Myanmar and the Maldives show high worry levels above 90% despite lower shares of deaths. In contrast, countries like New Zealand, the Netherlands, and Canada sit near the bottom of the chart with worry levels between 25% and 30% and very low shares of deaths. A cluster of European nations, including Germany, the United Kingdom, and Greece, occupies the lower-left area with worry levels between 40% and 60%. Asian countries display a wide spread; China shows moderate worry around 60% with minimal deaths, while Thailand and Kuwait show lower worry levels. The visualization highlights a general trend where higher shares of deaths often correlate with higher shares of worried respondents, though significant outliers exist where worry levels remain high despite lower mortality rates.
